prompt: What fundamentally changes when an AI goes from a tool you call to an agent that takes actions in your systems (restarts services, pushes config, runs commands)?
choices: Nothing changes; you operate it exactly like a read-only chatbot
The agent can no longer make mistakes because it has tools
The blast radius shifts from a wrong answer to a wrong action taken in production, so reliability becomes about bounding what the agent is allowed to do
Cost stops mattering because actions are free
explanation: A called tool returns text a human acts on; an agent takes the action itself, so a mistake is a wrong action in prod, not just a wrong answer. Operating it reliably is therefore about least privilege, dry-runs, and approval gates that bound the damage a confident-but-wrong action can cause.

prompt: Why instrument an action audit trail (what the agent did, what it observed, and why) rather than relying on your normal application dashboards?
choices: Because it removes the need for any human approval
Because application dashboards are always wrong
Because a green app dashboard won't tell you the agent took the wrong action, and a postmortem and an audit need the agent's actions and reasoning to hold a human accountable
Because the audit trail makes the agent run faster
explanation: '200 OK' tells you the service is up, not that the agent restarted the right one. You need a trail of the tools it called, what it observed, its reasoning, and the action taken — correlatable to the incident — so misfires are visible and a human stays accountable.

prompt: Which of the following are agent-specific failure modes you must operate against? (Select all that apply.)
choices: The monitoring dashboard being configured in dark mode
Confident-but-wrong remediation that acts on a wrong diagnosis and makes the incident worse
Looping — retrying a failing action forever, burning cost or repeating damage
Prompt injection via a crafted log line, ticket, or error message that steers the agent into a dangerous action
explanation: Loops, confident-but-wrong actions, and prompt injection through untrusted text the agent reads are all real failure modes once the AI can act on your systems. A dashboard's colour theme has no operational effect and isn't a failure mode.

prompt: How should you decide whether an ops agent may take an action autonomously or must get human approval first?
choices: By letting the agent decide its own permissions at runtime
By always requiring approval for everything, including reading logs
By how fast the action is to execute

By blast radius and reversibility: read-only and low-risk reversible actions can be autonomous, while destructive, irreversible, or production-facing actions need approval — backed by a kill-switch and a human accountable
explanation: Autonomy is set per action class by blast radius and reversibility. Reading logs or restarting a stateless service can be autonomous; deleting data or changing prod needs a human gate. A kill-switch and clear accountability backstop the whole scheme — gating even read-only actions would just add toil.

prompt: TRADE-OFF: You can give the on-call agent broad autonomy to act on most alerts (less human toil, faster resolution) or require human approval on most of its actions (slower, but a human reviews each one). What is the trade-off?
choices: There is no trade-off; full autonomy is always correct because it is fastest
Approval on everything is always mandatory, so autonomy can never be used
Both choices behave identically, so it only affects how the dashboard looks

Broad autonomy cuts toil and resolves incidents faster but widens the blast radius of a confident-but-wrong action; approval-gating is safer but slower and reintroduces human toil — the right level depends on each action's reversibility and blast radius
explanation: It's a real speed-vs-safety trade-off. Autonomy reduces toil and mean-time-to-resolution but lets a wrong action hit prod unreviewed; approval gates add safety at the cost of speed and human effort. You tune it per action class — autonomous for reversible low-blast actions, gated for destructive or irreversible ones.
